library.desc: The tskb package itself — its CLI, JSX runtime, and types for writing docs.

cli.desc: The command-line tool shipped by the package. Run as `tskb` after install. Builds the graph, runs queries, opens the explorer.

jsxRuntime.desc: A custom JSX runtime that turns .tskb.tsx files into a knowledge graph instead of DOM. Picked up by the docs tsconfig via `jsxImportSource: "tskb"`.

registry.desc: The global `namespace tskb` where Folders, Modules, Exports, and Terms are declared. All .tskb.tsx files share one registry — declarations merge across files.

graph.desc: The JSON output of the build: every node (folder, module, export, term, doc, flow) and the edges between them. Saved to `.tskb/graph.json`.

knowledgeChunk.desc: A JSON piece of the graph the SPA loads on demand. The meta chunk holds top-level info; one folder chunk per folder holds its contents.

explorerLane.desc: A horizontal band on the explorer canvas. Lanes group different kinds of nodes side by side.

nodeComponent.desc: The interface a node type implements to render itself on the canvas.

lruChunkCache.desc: A bounded cache inside the SPA. Keeps recently loaded chunks; drops the oldest when full.

explorerStaticExport.desc: A self-contained explorer folder that opens directly from the file system, no server needed.

ghostNode.desc: A placeholder node for a folder or file that exists on disk but isn't declared in the graph. Drawn as a faded card.

dotFile.desc: A Graphviz DOT file. Plain text describing nodes and edges. Used to draw the graph as a diagram with `dot -Tpng`.

tsProgram.desc: A TypeScript compiler `Program` built from source files and a tsconfig. tskb uses it to read the AST, types, and symbols of your code — without emitting JavaScript.

ast.desc: Abstract Syntax Tree. TypeScript's tree shape of source code.

declarationMerging.desc: TypeScript's rule that merges declarations with the same name. Lets the registry be split across files.

typeChecker.desc: TypeScript's component that resolves types and gives type info for symbols.

literalType.desc: A type that represents one specific value, like the string `'hello'`. Used to read constants out of type definitions.

jsxElement.desc:
A JSX node in the source tree, like `` or `

`.

typeAssertion.desc: TypeScript's `as` syntax. tskb uses it to point a `ref` at a registry entry: `ref as tskb.Modules['X']`.

depthFirstTraversal.desc: Tree traversal that follows each branch to its end before moving to the next. Used to walk JSX in order.

constantReferencesMap.desc: A lookup from each `const` in a doc file to the registry node it points to.

refCategoryMap.desc: A lookup from a JSX tag name to the registry category it belongs to.

globPattern.desc: A file-matching pattern like `**/*.tskb.tsx`. Used to pick which doc files to build.

tskbOutputDir.desc: The `.tskb/` folder. Holds the build output: `graph.json` and `graph.dot`.

searchResult.desc: A match returned by `tskb search`. A node plus a score from 0 to 1; higher means a better match.

resolvedVia.desc: How `resolveNode` found a match: by exact ID, by path, or by walking up to the nearest parent folder.

Module.desc: A generic helper to reference modules inside doc files

File.desc: A generic helper to reference non-JS/TS files (README.md, yml configs, etc.) inside doc files

ref.desc: A placeholder for referencing anything from the global tskb registry into jsx documentation tags

npm.README.md.desc: The main npm README.md for the tskb package
npm.README.md.content:
# tskb Let your **AI assistant document your codebase** as it works. Architecture knowledge accumulates in a **compiler-verified artifact** — query it, ask questions about your system, navigate it with your AI. > **Your AI writes the docs. You navigate them.** [![npm version](https://badge.fury.io/js/tskb.svg)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/tskb) [![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-blue.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT) --- ## See it work ```bash npm install --save-dev tskb npx tskb init npm run docs npx tskb explore ``` That's the setup — an empty graph, an interactive D3 explorer in the browser, and AI skills wired into Claude Code and GitHub Copilot. The actual codebase map fills in as you (and your AI) write `.tskb.tsx` docs over time; the skills walk the assistant through what to capture and how. --- ## Status **Beta** — `0.6.x`. The surface below is stable enough to build on: - **Stable:** CLI commands (`init`, `build`, `ls`, `pick`, `search`, `docs`, `flows`, `context`, `explore`), JSX components, registry primitives, graph schema, generated skill/instruction files. - **May still evolve:** internal extraction APIs, explorer chunk format, additional node types. Production-grade for documentation use. Pin a minor version if you script against the JSON output. --- ## What is tskb? A TypeScript-native DSL for expressing **architectural intent** as code. Your AI assistant writes and maintains documentation in `.tskb.tsx` files that: - are **type-checked by the TypeScript compiler** - reference **real folders, files, and exports** via `typeof import()` - fail the build when documentation and code drift apart The result is a **structured knowledge graph** you can query with the CLI, browse in the explorer, or feed to your AI assistant — all derived from the same source of truth. > Refactor your code → stale docs break at compile time. The AI fixes them. --- ## Why it exists Large TypeScript codebases drift in predictable ways: - **Tribal knowledge** — architecture lives in senior engineers' heads - **Documentation decay** — Markdown and diagrams silently fall out of sync - **Compound complexity** — onboarding cost grows faster than the codebase tskb makes architecture documentation **typed, validated, and enforced at build time** — so it can't quietly rot. --- ## Core concepts ### Vocabulary Declare structural elements once, in the global `tskb` namespace. Everything else references them. Six primitives cover the surface: | Primitive | What it represents | Validation | | ---------- | ------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | | `Folder` | A logical area (feature, layer, package) | `path` must exist on disk | | `Module` | A source file, with type-safe access to its shape | `typeof import("…")` | | `Export` | A named export from a module (class, fn, const) | `typeof import("…").Symbol` | | `File` | A non-TS/JS file (README, config, spec) | `path` must exist on disk | | `Term` | A domain concept not tied to a specific file | string literal type | | `External` | A third-party service or package (with metadata) | free-form key-value props | ```tsx // docs/vocabulary.tskb.tsx import type { Folder, Module, Export, File, Term, External } from "tskb"; declare global { namespace tskb { interface Folders { ServiceLayer: Folder<{ desc: "Business logic"; path: "src/services" }>; } interface Modules { AuthServiceModule: Module<{ desc: "Authentication service"; type: typeof import("../src/services/auth.service.js"); }>; } interface Exports { AuthService: Export<{ desc: "Authentication and authorization"; type: typeof import("../src/services/auth.service.js").AuthService; }>; } interface Files { "openapi-spec": File<{ desc: "REST API spec"; path: "docs/openapi.yml" }>; } interface Terms { jwt: Term<"JSON Web Tokens for stateless authentication">; } interface Externals { postgres: External<{ desc: "Primary relational database"; kind: "database"; url: "https://www.postgresql.org"; }>; } } } ``` `typeof import()` means the compiler validates every reference. Rename `AuthService` → the doc build breaks. ### Documents `.tskb.tsx` files reference the vocabulary and add prose, snippets, flows, relations, and architectural decisions. JSX components available: ``, `

` / `

` / `

`, `

`, `` / `

  • `, ``, `` / ``, ``, ``. ```tsx // docs/authentication.tskb.tsx import { Doc, H1, P, Snippet, ref } from "tskb"; import type { AuthService } from "../src/services/auth.service.js"; const Service = ref as tskb.Exports["AuthService"]; const Jwt = ref as tskb.Terms["jwt"]; export default (

    Authentication

    {Service} issues {Jwt} after validating credentials.

    { const auth: AuthService = new AuthService(); return auth.login({ email, password }); }} />     ); ``` `` is **not** a string literal — its body is fully type-checked. If `login()` changes signature, the doc build fails. ### Flows Named, ordered sequences through the system — login pipelines, build steps, request paths. They become first-class graph nodes. ```tsx ``` ### Relations and ADRs Express explicit semantic edges between any two nodes, and capture the _why_ behind decisions: ```tsx

    Stateless tokens let us scale the auth tier horizontally without sticky sessions.

     ``` `` adds a `related-to` edge to the graph; `` records an architectural decision as a queryable node so the rationale travels with the code. ### Priority Three levels control what the AI sees first: - `essential` — surfaced in generated AI skills and `ls` output - `constraint` — architectural rules; surface in `pick`/`search` so they can't be missed before changes - `supplementary` — graph-only, queryable on demand --- ## How the graph is built Edges come from two sources: **Authored intent** — every JSX reference creates a typed edge. `

    {AuthService} uses {UserRepository}

    ` records `Doc → AuthService` and `Doc → UserRepository`. `` adds explicit `related-to` edges. **Inferred structure** — folder hierarchy, module imports, export ownership, and file containment are read directly from the filesystem and TypeScript AST. The result combines what you _meant_ with what's _actually there_. --- ## CLI The build command runs the pipeline; the others query the graph. All commands accept `--plain` (~30% fewer tokens for AI consumption) and `--optimized` (compact JSON). ```bash npx tskb init # Interactive scaffolder npm run docs # Build the graph (added by init) npx tskb ls --depth 4 # Folder tree + essential docs npx tskb pick "AuthService" # Full detail for one node npx tskb search "auth" # Fuzzy search across the graph npx tskb context "ServiceLayer" # Node + neighborhood + docs (one call) npx tskb docs "auth" # Search documentation npx tskb flows # List flows by priority npx tskb explore # Open the interactive explorer npx tskb explore --export ./public # Bake static site ``` `context` is the single most efficient call — it returns the target node, its neighborhood, and all referencing docs (deduplicated, sorted by priority, with constraint docs surfaced) in one shot. --- ## Explorer `tskb explore` serves a D3-powered SPA that renders the graph as an interactive diagram — folders nest, modules expand, edges trace references and imports. The SPA ships pre-built; no extra install. `--export` writes a self-contained static folder you can drop on any host. --- ## AI assistant integration The build emits skills/instructions for two assistants. Content is regenerated from the graph on every build — never edit the files manually. Claude Code gets four split skills (load only what's needed); Copilot gets two consolidated instruction files. | Skill | Purpose | Generated files | | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **tskb** | Codebase map and CLI orientation. Always-on entry point. | `.claude/skills/tskb/SKILL.md`
    `.github/instructions/tskb.instructions.md` | | **tskb-toc** | Repo table of contents: boundaries, externals, flows, essential-docs index. Loaded on demand. | `.claude/skills/tskb-toc/SKILL.md` | | **tskb-update** | When and where to write `.tskb.tsx` docs — workflow, folder structure, authoring rules. | `.claude/skills/tskb-update/SKILL.md`
    `.github/instructions/tskb-update.instructions.md` | | **tskb-update-syntax** | Full authoring syntax reference: registry primitives, JSX components, snippets, class methods. | `.claude/skills/tskb-update-syntax/SKILL.md` | Enable them during `npx tskb init` or create `.claude/skills/` and `.github/` before the next build. The loop: at the start of a session the assistant already has the folder tree and essential docs; for anything deeper it runs `search`, `context`, or `pick` itself. Less exploration, more accuracy. ### Building docs iteratively The skills are designed to compound — each session adds to the graph, and the next session sees what's already there. A typical loop: > **You:** "I'm adding JWT auth in `src/services/auth.service.ts`. Document it." > > **AI** (via `tskb-update`): > > 1. Runs `tskb search "auth"` and `tskb context "src/services"` — checks what's already mapped, avoids duplicating. > 2. Reads the new code and proposes the questions worth answering: _"How does login issue a JWT?"_, _"What guarantees token expiry?"_, _"Why is `bcrypt.compare` constant-time-required here?"_ > 3. You pick which questions are real. The AI declares any new vocabulary (`AuthService`, `jwt`) in the registry, then writes one `.tskb.tsx` per question. > 4. Runs `npm run docs`. The TypeScript compiler validates every `typeof import()` reference and every ``. > > **Next session, in a different feature:** the AI runs `tskb search` first, finds the auth doc, and references `AuthService` directly — no re-reading the code, no contradictory descriptions. Each iteration adds nodes, flows, and constraints. The graph gets denser over time, and the AI's answers get sharper because it's reading curated map fragments instead of grep results. --- ## Output schema ```ts { nodes: { folders, modules, exports, files, terms, flows, docs // typed records }, edges: Array<{ from: string; to: string; type: "references" | "contains" | "belongs-to" | "imports" | "related-to" | "flow-step"; label?: string; }>, metadata: { generatedAt, version, stats } } ``` The graph is the primary output. The explorer, Graphviz DOT (`.tskb/graph.dot`), and AI skill files are all derived from it. --- ## CI / pre-commit Treat the doc build like a type check — if it fails, code and docs have drifted. ```bash # .husky/pre-commit npm run docs ``` ```yaml # GitHub Actions - run: npm run docs - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3 with: { name: architecture-graph, path: .tskb/ } ``` --- ## How it differs **vs Markdown / ADRs** — type-checked against real code, not free text that drifts. **vs Structurizr / C4 / PlantUML** — native TypeScript instead of a custom DSL; produces a queryable graph, not just static diagrams. **vs TypeDoc** — documents _architecture and intent_ (why things exist, how they relate), not API surfaces. Unique to tskb: compiler-validated references via `typeof import()`, type-checked code snippets, first-class flows, doc priority, an interactive explorer, and AI skills generated from the graph. --- ## Roadmap - Architectural constraint validation (beyond `priority="constraint"` surfacing) - **First-class JSX for common architecture patterns** — purpose-built components for `` (with typed `` children and one-way dependency rules), `` (``, ``, `` with direction enforcement), `` (``, ``, ``, ``), `` (``, ``, ``, ``), `` (``, ``), `` (``, ``, ``), and pipeline / saga / pub-sub variants. Each emits typed graph nodes and edges, surfaces violations at build time, and renders distinctly in the explorer. - More AI assistant integrations --- ## License MIT © Dimitar Mihaylov

docs/src/tskb/usage.tskb.tsx.explains: What's the typical workflow for setting up tskb in a repo?
docs/src/tskb/usage.tskb.tsx.content:
Adopting tskb in a project follows a small fixed sequence: configure, declare, write, build.

  • Create a docs/ folder for .tskb.tsx files.
  • Add a tsconfig like sample.tsconfig.jsonpointing at the repo root with jsx: "react-jsx" and jsxImportSource: "tskb" .
  • Declare structural elements (Folders, Modules, Exports, Terms, Files) using global namespace augmentation. Use typeof import() for type-safe references.
  • Write *.tskb.tsx files using the JSX components. TypeScript validates every reference at compile time.
  • Run cli.buildvia the cli: tskb "**/*.tskb.tsx" --tsconfig tsconfig.json .

docs/src/tskb/tests.tskb.tsx.explains: How is the tskb CLI validated end-to-end?
docs/src/tskb/tests.tskb.tsx.content:
E2E Test Infrastructure

The test suite ( tests) validates the entire tskb CLI by running it as a subprocess against a realistic fixture project. Tests use Vitest and spawn the built CLI binary via Node's execFileSync, asserting on stdout and generated artifacts.

Developer runs `npm test`: Vitest loads config, global setup builds the fixture graph, test files exercise every CLI command, teardown cleans output

  1. 1
    vitest.configdiscovers test files, registers global setup
  2. 2
    tests.global-setupbuilds fixture graph once via tskb build
  3. 3
    tests.helpersprovides CLI runners and graph loader to all test files
  4. 4
    tests.global-setupteardown cleans .tskb/ output directory

`init.test.ts` runs: copies fixture to a temp dir, invokes `tskb init`, asserts the generated files

  1. 1
    tests.initorchestrates the test: arranges temp dir, runs init, asserts output
  2. 2
    tests.helperscopies fixture src/ into the temp directory
  3. 3
    cli.buildruns tskb init --yes in the temp directory
  4. 4
    tests.initasserts scaffolded files: tsconfig, starter doc, scripts, AI dirs

Fixture Project

The fixture at tests.e2e.fixtureis a small task-management TypeScript app with models, services, API routes, and utilities. It includes its own tskb docs folder with 6 doc files covering all three priority levels (essential, constraint, supplementary) and all registry primitives (Folder, Module, Export, Term, External, Relation, Flow). This makes it a comprehensive test subject for the full build and query pipeline.

Test Structure

Tests are split into focused files under tests.e2e, sharing utilities from tests.helpers. A Vitest global setup ( tests.global-setup) builds the fixture graph once before any test runs.

  • tests.init— scaffolding tests with idempotency checks
  • tests.build— graph output validation: node counts, priorities, edges
  • tests.commands— exercises every query command in plain and JSON modes
  • tests.disambiguation— ambiguous ID resolution across commands
  • tests.graph-integrity— edge consistency, type signatures, external metadata

Running Tests

Tests require the package to be built first (npm run build). Run with npm test (vitest run) or npm run test:watch (vitest) for interactive mode. The 60-second timeout in vitest.configaccounts for CLI subprocess startup cost.

docs/src/tskb/primitives.tskb.tsx.explains: What primitives does tskb provide for writing documentation?
docs/src/tskb/primitives.tskb.tsx.content:
The library exports a small set of registry primitives for declaring structural elements, plus JSX components from Jsx.runtime.jsfor writing the prose around them.

  • Folder— logical groupings in the codebase (features, layers, packages).
  • Module— concrete code units (source files).
  • Export— type-safe reference to a named export. Uses typeof import() so the compiler validates the symbol exists.
  • Term— domain concepts and patterns not tied to a specific file.
  • File— non-JS/TS files (README.md, yml configs, etc.) referenced by path.
  • ref— placeholder for referencing any registered node from JSX via a type assertion.

JSX components from Jsx.runtime.js(Doc, H1, H2, H3, P, List, Li, Snippet, Flow, Step, Relation, Adr) provide the structured-content syntax around those references.

docs/src/tskb/main.tskb.tsx.content:
tskb

A TypeScript DSL for type-safe architectural documentation. TSKB.Package.Rootcontains the library, CLI, and pre-built explorer SPA. Its package.jsondeclares a tskb bin that runs the cli, and npm.README.mdis the public-facing readme.

Two main entry modules sit alongside the cli: Main.index.js(the library API) and Jsx.runtime.js(the JSX runtime, jsxRuntime).

docs/src/tskb/runtime/runtime.tskb.tsx.explains: What does the runtime module provide and what does it not do?
docs/src/tskb/runtime/runtime.tskb.tsx.content:
Runtime

Located in tskb.runtime. Contains registry type definitions and JSX primitives - no actual runtime execution.

Modules

  • runtime.jsx: JSX runtime with jsx.Doc(requires explains prop and optional priority via DocPriority: essential, constraint, or supplementary). Includes heading/paragraph/list components, jsx.Adrfor Architecture Decision Records, and jsx.reffor type-safe registry references.
  • runtime.jsxalso provides jsx.Flowand jsx.Stepfor defining named, ordered sequences through the system. Flows become first-class graph nodes with priority support. Only Step children are allowed inside Flow (validated at build time).
  • runtime.registry: Type definitions for Folder, Module, Export, Term interfaces used in global namespace augmentation.

Purpose

Provides TypeScript types and JSX functions for authoring *.tskb.tsx files. Registry interfaces enable type-safe vocabulary declarations. JSX components structure documentation content.

Semantic Relations

The Relation tag creates a semantic edge in the knowledge graph between two nodes. It is type-safe and supports any registered Folder, Module, Term, or Export. The optional label prop allows you to describe the relationship (e.g., "depends on").

This will emit a related-to edge from the runtime.jsx module to the Graph term in the TSKB graph, with the label "runtime describes graph".

docs/src/tskb/explorer/transform.tskb.ts.explains: How does the KnowledgeGraph become the chunks the explorer SPA loads?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/transform.tskb.ts.content:
Graph Transform

explorer.transformis the single step between the raw knowledge graph and what the browser receives. explorer.transformGraphis implemented as a class ( GraphToExplorerTransformer ) whose transform() method orchestrates all steps — edge indexing, meta assembly, folder chunk recursion, ghost injection, and child count patching.

Output shape

The output is ExplorerChunks : a MetaChunk plus a Map<folderId, FolderChunk> . The server and exporter serialize each entry to a separate knowledgeChunkJSON file: meta.json and folder-{sanitizedId}.json .

MetaChunk contains: root folder node, top-level folder summaries (with _hasChildren / _childCount hints), all doc nodes, flow nodes, term nodes, external nodes, and cross-lane references edges.

FolderChunk contains: direct sub-folders, direct modules, all exports of those modules, import edges between modules in this folder ( internalEdges ), and import edges that cross the folder boundary ( externalEdges ).

Edge lookup indexes

Before anything else, the transformer builds two Map<nodeId, GraphEdge[]> indexes — one keyed by the source node id ( edgesBySource ) and one by the target node id ( edgesByTarget ). All subsequent queries run in O(1) per node instead of scanning the full edge list.

Recursive folder chunking

buildAllFolderChunksRecursively() does a depth-first walk starting from the root folder, following contains edges. For each folder it finds direct modules (via belongs-to edges pointing at the folder) and direct sub-folders (via contains edges from the folder). Folders with neither are skipped but still recursed into, so deeply nested folders with content are always reached.

Ghost node injection

Ghost injection runs in two passes. First, buildGhostIntermediaryChains() detects path gaps between a declared folder's path and its owned modules or sub-folders. When a module lives at pkg/src/core/server.ts but its declared parent folder has path pkg , the transformer synthesizes ghostNodeFolderChunk entries for every intermediate directory ( pkg/src , pkg/src/core ) and re-parents the module to the deepest one. The chain is not synthesized if any intermediate path is already owned by a declared folder.

Second, injectGhostNodes() scans every declared folder's folder.children.files and folder.children.folders (scanner data recorded during tskb build ). Any .ts / .tsx file not already in a chunk becomes a ghost module node. Any direct child directory not already represented (either as a declared folder or a ghost intermediary) and not a transparent intermediary becomes a ghost folder node. Ghost intermediary chunks are not processed by this pass — they only contain what the graph already knows.

Child count patching

After ghost injection is complete, patchFolderChildCounts() writes the final _hasChildren and _childCount detail fields on every folder ExplorerNode . These fields drive the expand button visibility and badge in the SPA. The patch runs last so ghost nodes are included in the counts.

The explorer asks transformGraph to chunk a KnowledgeGraph for delivery: edges are indexed, the meta chunk is built, folder chunks are walked, ghosts are filled in, then counts and ordering are finalised

    explorer.transformGraphentry point: instantiates the transformer, indexes edges by source and target, then runs the phases below
  2. 2
    explorer.transformbuilds the meta chunk: root folder, top folders, docs, flows, terms, externals, and cross-lane edges
  3. 3
    explorer.transformrecursively builds one folder chunk per folder with its direct modules, exports, sub-folders, and import edges (split into internal vs external)
  4. 4
    explorer.transformfills in ghost intermediary folders for path gaps between a declared folder and its modules, then injects ghost nodes for undeclared files and folders found by the scanner
  5. 5
    explorer.transformpatches folder child counts so the SPA can show expand affordances, sorts chunk contents alphabetically, and builds the parent-of map

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa.tskb.tsx.explains: What are the SPA's main layers and how do they fit together?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer SPA

The SPA is built with Vite (D3 in a separate vendor chunk) and lives in the explorer-app/ directory. It is pre-built into dist/explorer/ and shipped inside the tskb npm package. explorer.spa.mainis the entry point that bootstraps the canvas, wires all interaction handlers, and drives the render loop.

The SPA is split into five layers, each documented separately:

  • Data layer ( explorer.spa.chunk-types) — loads and caches chunks of the graph on demand.
  • Layout ( explorer.spa.lane-engine) — arranges visible nodes into three horizontal lanes (structure, docs, terms/flows).
  • Nodes, edges, and boundaries ( explorer.spa.node-base) — draws the cards, parent– child edges, and architectural boundary outlines.
  • Tooltips and spinners ( explorer.spa.node-tooltip) — hover, code-preview, and per-node loading overlays that track their anchors as the canvas pans and zooms.
  • Doc panel ( explorer.spa.doc-panel) — slide-in side panel that hosts a stack of addressable views, synced to location.hash .

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-tooltips.tskb.explains: How do tooltips and spinners track canvas nodes as they pan and zoom?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-tooltips.tskb.content:
Tooltips and spinners

Spinners

explorer.spa.spinnerprovides a full-screen global spinner (shown while meta.json loads) and a per-node inline spinner: explorer.spa.showNodeSpinnerappends an animated SVG ring at the node's canvas position; explorer.spa.removeNodeSpinnerremoves it in the finally block after the chunk fetch settles.

Hover and code tooltips

explorer.spa.node-tooltipappears on hover, anchored to the node's right-center SVG position. explorer.spa.code-tooltipis a click-toggled code preview popup anchored to the node's top-center SVG position. Both receive updateXxxTransform() calls from explorer.spa.mainon every D3 zoom event so they reposition to track their anchor nodes as the canvas pans and zooms.

DOM tooltip

explorer.spa.dom-tooltipis anchored to a DOM element rather than an SVG node. It is used by explorer.spa.doc-panelto show a small popup on a.tskb-ref hover with the referenced node's path, type color, and description.

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-panel.tskb.ts.explains: How does the doc panel host and route addressable views?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-panel.tskb.ts.content:
Doc panel and router

Panel host

explorer.spa.DocPanelin explorer.spa.doc-panelis the detail side panel. It is a thin shell — it subscribes to explorer.spa.panelRouterand, on each stack change, clears its header and body slots then delegates rendering entirely to the active View . It owns no view-specific markup, no deps, and no wiring logic.

Router and views

explorer.spa.Routermaintains a stack of View instances. Callers construct a view with its own deps and call push(view) ; the router appends it to the stack, notifies subscribers, and (when hash sync is on) writes the route to location.hash . explorer.spa.Router.registerViewserves a separate purpose: it binds a factory closure used only for hash restoration — when hashchange fires the router calls the factory to reconstruct a view from the URL fragment without needing a caller.

Boot wiring (run once in mount): 

const deps = {
          getNode: () => undefined,
          getRefsFor: () => [],
          onNodeRef: () => {},
          onNodeHighlight: () => {},
          onNodePrefetch: async () => {},
        };

        // Shell subscribes — every stack change re-renders the panel
        new DocPanel(panelRouter);

        // Bind deps into a factory closure for hash restoration only
        panelRouter.registerView(RefsView, deps);

        // Start hash sync; restores any view encoded in location.hash
        panelRouter.init({ syncHash: true });

Each view is instantiated fresh per navigation with its own nodeId , kind, and explorer.spa.NodeRefHooks— immutable after construction. Shared types ( View , ViewContext ) live in explorer.spa.router-types.

Reusable components

explorer.spa.renderAccordionin explorer.spa.accordionproduces the

accordion markup shared across views. explorer.spa.wireRefsin explorer.spa.ref-linksattaches the hover/click/prefetch behavior to every a.tskb-ref under a root element. Views call explorer.spa.wireRefsthemselves at the end of each render — explorer.spa.doc-panelhas no knowledge of it. Hover shows a explorer.spa.dom-tooltipwith the node's path, type color, and description; if the node's chunk isn't loaded yet, a background prefetch fires and the tooltip updates in place when data arrives.

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-nodes.tskb.ts.explains: How are nodes, edges, and boundaries rendered on the canvas?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-nodes.tskb.ts.content:
Nodes, edges, and boundaries

Node components

explorer.spa.node-indexis the extension point for node rendering: explorer.spa.createNodeRenderer accepts all interaction callbacks and returns a nodeComponent. Currently always delegates to explorer.spa.BaseNodeRenderer; add a type-dispatch switch here when a node type needs its own renderer. explorer.spa.node-basedefines the nodeComponentinterface and explorer.spa.BaseNodeRenderer— the default renderer for all node types. Each node is an SVG <g> containing a rounded-rect card, type icon, label, description, edge-count badge, a <> code preview bubble (top-center), and a + expand bubble (right edge). ghostNodes render as dashed transparent placeholders showing only their filename.

Edges

explorer.spa.edge-rendererderives parent→child links via explorer.spa.buildStructureLinksand draws them as horizontal cubic-bezier SVG paths via explorer.spa.renderStructureEdges. Ghost links (where either endpoint has detail._ghost = 'true' ) render with a dashed stroke and reduced opacity. It also renders the labeled background bands for each lane.

Boundaries

explorer.spa.boundary-rendererdraws architectural boundary outlines. When a folder node carries detail.boundary , all visible descendants (resolved by walking parentOf ) are grouped under that boundary name. explorer.spa.renderBoundaryGroups computes a convex hull over the bounding boxes of all member nodes, perturbs the hull points with a seeded pseudo-random wobble (stable across re-renders), and smooths the result with d3.curveBasisClosed for a hand-drawn look. The outline renders as a violet dashed stroke with a near-transparent fill and a pill label at the top. All elements have pointer-events: none .

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-layout.tskb.t.explains: How does the lane engine arrange nodes into three lanes on the canvas?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-layout.tskb.t.content:
Three-lane layout

explorer.spa.lane-enginecomputes all SVG positions in a single pass. The canvas has three explorerLanes stacked vertically:

  • Structure — builds a d3.hierarchy from the visible tree (root → expanded folders → modules → exports, including sub-folder nesting). Runs d3.tree().nodeSize() for left-to-right positioning. Collapsed folders with _childCount > 0 inject ghost placeholder nodes so the tree pre-reserves space for their children.
  • Docs — horizontal list ordered by priority (essential → constraint → supplementary).
  • Terms / Flows — horizontal list below docs.

On every expand or collapse, explorer.spa.maincalls explorer.spa.computeLayoutand fully re-renders the D3 selection with enter/update/exit.

docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-data.tskb.tsx.explains: How does the SPA load and cache graph chunks?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/spa-data.tskb.tsx.content:
Data layer

explorer.spa.chunk-typesdefines the chunk discriminated union ( MetaChunk | FolderChunk ) and the ChunkRegistry map that keys chunk kinds to their types. Adding a new chunk kind means extending this file only.

explorer.spa.loaderprovides explorer.spa.ChunkLoader: a generic load(kind, ...args) whose return type is inferred from ChunkRegistry[K] . It deduplicates in-flight requests and uses a lruChunkCache(max 50 entries) so folders are not re-fetched on collapse/expand cycles.

explorer.spa.GraphStorein explorer.spa.storeholds only graph data (meta chunk, folder chunks map). Data arrives via loadMeta() and loadFolderChunk() . UI state — the expanded node set, selected node, and search query — lives as plain variables in explorer.spa.main and is passed explicitly to explorer.spa.computeLayout, explorer.spa.renderStructureEdges, and explorer.spa.BaseNodeRenderer. The store exposes subscribe(listener) for reactive re-render.

docs/src/tskb/explorer/serve.tskb.tsx.explains: How does the explorer HTTP server serve chunks and SPA assets?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/serve.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer Server

explorer.serveExplorerin explorer.serverstarts a Node built-in http server — no external dependency. It runs the graph transform once at startup, serializes every knowledgeChunk to JSON, and keeps all of them in a Map<string, string> for the lifetime of the process. Subsequent chunk requests are pure string writes with no re-serialization.

Request routing

Two routes are handled:

/chunks/*.json — chunk API. The key is extracted from the pathname (e.g. meta or folder-tskb.core ) and looked up in the in-memory cache. A 404 is returned if the key is unknown.

/* (static assets) — SPA files are served from dist/explorer/ , resolved via import.meta.url so the path stays correct after npm pack . Any unknown path falls back to index.html so the SPA router handles client-side navigation. A path traversal guard rejects any request that resolves outside dist/explorer/ .

Command entry point

explorer.explorein cli.commands.explorelocates graph.json via cli.utils.graph-finder, parses it, then branches: if --export is provided it calls exportExplorer ; otherwise it calls explorer.serveExplorer. The module is dynamically imported so the SPA assets directory is only resolved when actually needed.

Developer runs `tskb explore`: the CLI loads the graph, transforms it into chunks, starts an HTTP server, and the browser loads the SPA and fetches chunks on demand

  1. 1
    explorer.exploreloads the graph file and routes to the serve path
  2. 2
    explorer.transformGraphconverts the KnowledgeGraph into a MetaChunk and one FolderChunk per folder, held in memory
  3. 3
    explorer.serveExplorerstarts the HTTP server: chunk routes serve the in-memory chunks as JSON; everything else serves the SPA assets
  4. 4
    explorer.spa.mainExplorerApp mounts on page load: sets up canvas, renderer, and search, then fetches the meta chunk
  5. 5
    explorer.spa.lane-enginepositions all visible nodes across the three lanes
  6. 6
    explorer.spa.node-baseD3 enter/update renders node cards into SVG groups

docs/src/tskb/explorer/export.tskb.tsx.explains: How does the explorer export to a static directory that opens from file://?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/export.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer Static Export

explorer.exportExplorerin explorer.exportproduces a explorerStaticExport: a directory containing the pre-built SPA ( index.html + hashed JS/CSS assets) and all knowledgeChunkJSON files written to a chunks/ sub-directory.

Output layout

The default output directory is .tskb/explorer/ . The structure mirrors what the server serves dynamically:

index.html , assets/ — Vite-built SPA with content-hashed filenames.

chunks/meta.json — MetaChunk: root, top-level folders, docs, flows, terms, externals.

chunks/folder-{sanitizedId}.json — one FolderChunk per folder with modules or sub-folders.

File:// compatibility

The SPA fetches chunks using relative paths ( ./chunks/meta.json ) rather than absolute server routes. This means the exported output works when opened directly in a browser via file:// without running a server, making it suitable for archiving or sharing as a zip.

Developer runs the explorer export command: the graph file is read, chunks are transformed in memory, then the pre-built SPA and chunk files are written to the output directory

  1. 1
    explorer.explorereads the graph file and resolves the output directory from the export flag
  2. 2
    explorer.transformGraphconverts KnowledgeGraph into a MetaChunk plus one FolderChunk per folder, held in memory
  3. 3
    explorer.exportExplorercopies the pre-built SPA into the output directory and writes all chunk JSON files alongside it

docs/src/tskb/explorer/explorer.tskb.tsx.explains: What is the tskb explorer and how does its data flow from CLI to browser?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/explorer.tskb.tsx.content:
Explorer

The explorer is a D3-powered SPA that renders the knowledge graph as a visual, interactive diagram. It opens via tskb explore (local HTTP server) or exports to a self-contained static folder via tskb explore --export .

The implementation splits into two layers: the CLI-side layer in tskb.explorer.core(graph transform, HTTP server, static export) and the Vite SPA in tskb.explorer.app(D3 canvas, chunk loading, layout, node components).

CLI layer

The CLI layer has three focused modules — see their dedicated docs for details:

explorer.transform( explorer.transformGraph) — converts a flat KnowledgeGraph into ExplorerChunks : a MetaChunk plus one FolderChunk per folder that has modules or sub-folders. Ghost nodes are injected for filesystem files not declared in the graph.

explorer.server( explorer.serveExplorer) — Node built-in http server that caches all chunk JSON strings in memory after the first transform and serves them at /chunks/*.json . No external server dependency.

explorer.export( explorer.exportExplorer) — copies the pre-built SPA assets to an output directory and writes all chunk JSON files alongside them, producing a fully self-contained static explorer.

Build integration

npm run build:explorer runs Vite, outputting to dist/explorer/ . npm run build:lib runs tsc , outputting to dist/ . The full npm run build runs explorer first, then lib — so the SPA assets are inside dist/ when the package is published.

During development, npm run dev:explorer starts Vite's HMR dev server against a fixed chunks/ directory. The CLI build:lib must be run at least once so the dist/ server code exists.

docs/src/tskb/explorer/app.tskb.tsx.explains: How does the ExplorerApp class boot, render, and respond to interaction?
docs/src/tskb/explorer/app.tskb.tsx.content:
ExplorerApp

explorer.spa.ExplorerAppin explorer.spa.mainis the single top-level controller for the explorer SPA. All canvas layers, UI state, and callbacks are owned by the class instance. The only public surface is explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.mount— everything else is private, wired internally via callbacks.

Boot sequence

explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.mountruns four synchronous setup methods then awaits data. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupCanvas creates the SVG layer stack and wires zoom/pan. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupTooltipsmounts hover and code tooltips. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupRendererconstructs the node renderer with bound interaction callbacks. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupSearchwires the search input. explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.loadInitialDatafetches meta.json and calls store.loadMeta() , which notifies the render subscriber and triggers the first explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.render.

Render loop

explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.renderis a pure coordinator: it recomputes the layout when dirty, delegates all application logic to explorer.spa.computeRenderStatein explorer.spa.render-state, then passes the result straight to the D3 rendering functions. No computation happens inside explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.renderitself — the boundary between logic and rendering is explorer.spa.render-state.

Interaction handlers

explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.onExpandhandles folder and module expand/collapse. For folder expand it calls explorer.spa.showNodeSpinner, fetches via the loader, calls explorer.spa.removeNodeSpinnerin finally , then updates the expanded set and calls explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.render. Collapse delegates to explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.collapseDescendantsto recursively clear all descendants from the expanded set.

ExplorerApp.mount(): four setup phases then meta fetch that triggers the first render

  1. 1
    explorer.spa.ExplorerAppnew ExplorerApp().mount() called on page load
  2. 2
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupCanvasAppends zoom-layer → lane-bg-layer / boundary-layer / edge-layer / node-layer; configures d3.zoom with tooltip transform propagation
  3. 3
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupTooltipsmountNodeTooltip() and mountCodeTooltip() attach DOM tooltip elements anchored to the SVG
  4. 4
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupRenderercreateNodeRenderer() wires onExpand / onSelect / onTraceLinks / hasChildren / code preview callbacks to ExplorerApp instance methods
  5. 5
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.setupSearchSearch input 'input' event updates searchQuery and calls render()
  6. 6
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.loadInitialDataloader.load('meta') fetches /chunks/meta.json; global spinner shown while pending
  7. 7
    explorer.spa.storestore.loadMeta() stores the chunk and notifies the render subscriber
  8. 8
    explorer.spa.lane-engineFirst render(): computeLayout positions top-folder nodes across the Structure lane

A store update or interaction calls ExplorerApp.render(): layout, edges, D3 join, search dim

  1. 1
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.rendertriggered by store subscription or a direct call from an interaction handler
  2. 2
    explorer.spa.computeLayoutcomputeLayout(store, expanded): builds d3.hierarchy from visible tree, runs d3.tree for left-to-right positions. Result cached until layoutDirty is set.
  3. 3
    explorer.spa.computeRenderStatecomputeRenderState(store, layout, searchQuery): derives allNodes, canvasW, structureLinks, relationLinks, matchIds. Pure — no D3.
  4. 4
    explorer.spa.edge-rendererrenderLaneBands(): draws labeled background bands for Structure and Externals lanes
  5. 5
    explorer.spa.buildStructureLinksbuildStructureLinks(structureNodes): derives parent→child StructureLink pairs from parentId fields
  6. 6
    explorer.spa.renderStructureEdgesrenderStructureEdges(): redraws cubic-bezier SVG paths; ghost links get dashed stroke
  7. 7
    explorer.spa.node-baseD3 enter/update/exit: new node cards appended, merged nodes repositioned, removed nodes fade out; search dim sets 0.15 on non-matching nodes

ExplorerApp.onExpand(): fetch chunk if needed, update expanded set, re-render

  1. 1
    explorer.spa.node-baseUser clicks + button; BaseNodeRenderer fires the onExpand callback
  2. 2
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.onExpandIf already expanded: collapseDescendants() clears the subtree, calls render()
  3. 3
    explorer.spa.showNodeSpinnerIf chunk not cached: showNodeSpinner() appends animated SVG ring next to the node
  4. 4
    explorer.spa.loaderloader.load('folder', id): cache hit or fetches /chunks/folder-{sanitizedId}.json
  5. 5
    explorer.spa.removeNodeSpinnerremoveNodeSpinner() called in finally — always removes spinner whether fetch succeeded or failed
  6. 6
    explorer.spa.storestore.loadFolderChunk() stores chunk and notifies subscribers
  7. 7
    explorer.spa.ExplorerApp.renderrender() called after expanded set update — new nodes enter, layout recomputed

docs/src/tskb/core/visualization.tskb.ts.explains: How is the knowledge graph rendered into a Graphviz DOT file?
docs/src/tskb/core/visualization.tskb.ts.content:
Visualization

Located in core.visualization. Contains visualization.dot-generatorwhich provides generateDot. Transforms a KnowledgeGraph into Graphviz DOT format — folders become nested subgraphs, modules/exports are grouped within their folders, terms and docs get separate clusters. Output is written to .tskb/graph.dot during build.

docs/src/tskb/core/typescript.tskb.tsx.explains: How does tskb create a TypeScript Program for static analysis without compiling?
docs/src/tskb/core/typescript.tskb.tsx.content:
TypeScript Program

Lives under tskb.coreas the typescript area. typescript.programexports ts.createProgram which creates a tsProgramfor analyzing code without compilation (noEmit=true).

  • Reads and parses tsconfig.json for compiler options
  • Validates TypeScript errors in specified files
  • Returns Program with AST, type checker, and symbol resolution

`tskb build` invokes createProgram to set up TypeScript static analysis, then hands the Program to extraction and graph assembly

  1. 1
    ts.createProgramcreates Program from tsconfig + source files
  2. 2
    extraction.registrywalks AST for Folders, Modules, Terms, Exports
  3. 3
    extraction.documentationparses JSX trees for content and references
  4. 4
    graph.builderassembles nodes and edges into knowledge graph

docs/src/tskb/core/registry-extraction.t.explains: How does tskb extract Folders, Modules, Exports, and Terms from the TypeScript AST?
docs/src/tskb/core/registry-extraction.t.content:
Registry Extraction

extractRegistrywalks the TypeScript AST for registry declarations, then enriches each module with its public API shape via extraction.module-morphology.

During `tskb build`, extractRegistry enriches every Module declaration with its public API: exports, imports, and type stubs

  1. 1
    extractRegistrycollects Module declarations from tskb namespace
  2. 2
    extractModuleMorphologyresolves source file, classifies exports, renders code stubs
  3. 3
    extractModuleImportswalks import declarations for symbols and paths
  4. 4
    graph.builderassembles enriched ModuleNode with edges

docs/src/tskb/core/graph-builder.tskb.ts.explains: How is the knowledge graph modeled and assembled from extracted data?
docs/src/tskb/core/graph-builder.tskb.ts.content:
Graph Module

Located in core.extraction.graph. Takes extracted registry and documentation, produces a KnowledgeGraph.

Modules

  • graph.types: Defines the graph data model. Node types: FolderNode, ModuleNode, TermNode, ExportNode, FlowNode, DocNode. Edge types: references, belongs-to, contains, related-to, flow-step. Top-level structure: KnowledgeGraphwith nodes, edges, and metadata.
  • graph.builder: buildGraphcreates nodes from registry items and docs, then builds edges — explicit references from docs and inferred hierarchy from folder paths (contains/belongs-to).

docs/src/tskb/core/docs-extraction.tskb..explains: How does tskb turn .tskb.tsx files into HTML, references, and graph edges?
docs/src/tskb/core/docs-extraction.tskb..content:
Documentation Extraction

extraction.documentationis the second extraction pass in the build pipeline. extractDocs receives the TypeScript Program and the extraction.registryoutput, and produces an ExtractedDocfor each .tskb.tsx file. Each result carries the HTML content string, the reference arrays used to build graph edges, and any embedded jsx.Flowand declarations.

Two-pass strategy per file

Each file is processed by extractFromTsxFilein two steps. First, buildRefMap walks all variable declarations and builds the constantReferencesMap— a lookup from local const names like AuthModule to their registry key. This lets the traversal resolve inline {AuthModule} expressions without re-parsing the typeAssertion. Second, JsxExtractoris instantiated with the map and performs depthFirstTraversalover the default export JSX tree, accumulating HTML and references.

Reference resolution

References appear in three forms: explicit tag elements ( , , etc.), inline identifier expressions ( {AuthModule} ), and inline type assertions ( {ref as tskb.Modules['auth']} ). All three routes converge on buildRefLinkvia the refCategoryMapdispatch table inside JsxExtractor.

buildRefLinkembeds data-node-type and data-node-display attributes pre-computed from extraction.registryso the explorer doc panel can render tooltips for unloaded nodes before their chunk is fetched.

Flow and Relation extraction

jsx.Flowelements are validated inside JsxExtractor— only jsx.Step children are allowed, text nodes throw. Each jsx.Stepnode attribute is resolved through constantReferencesMap, then resolveNodeMetalooks up the node type and display string from the registry so every step link carries the same pre-computed attributes as regular refs. Validated flows are pushed into the flows[] array of ExtractedDocand also emitted as an HTML

block.

Single .tskb.tsx file through extraction: constant map → JSX traversal → HTML + references

  1. 1
    extractDocsIterates Program source files; dispatches matched .tsx files to extractFromTsxFile
  2. 2
    extractFromTsxFileBuilds constant ref map, finds default export, instantiates JsxExtractor
  3. 3
    buildRefMapWalks all variable declarations; maps const names → {category, name} via type-assertion parsing
  4. 4
    extraction.registryPassed in as optional param; queried by buildRefLink to pre-compute data-node-display values
  5. 5
    JsxExtractorDepth-first JSX traversal via visitElement dispatch: structural tags → HTML, XxxRef / inline refs → buildRefLink; Flow and Relation elements validated and collected
  6. 6
    buildRefLinkEmits <a class=tskb-ref data-node-id data-node-type data-node-display>; pushes id into refs sub-array
  7. 7
    resolveNodeMetaCalled per Flow step: probes all registry maps to return {nodeType, display} for the step anchor
  8. 8
    ExtractedDocReturned: filePath, HTML content, references arrays, flows[], relations[]
  9. 9
    graph.builderConsumes ExtractedDoc: creates DocNode + FlowNode entries and references/flow-step edges

docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-tes.explains: What test coverage is required for new functionality in the tskb package?
docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-tes.content:
Any new behaviour added to the tskb package must ship with E2E coverage in tests. The suite runs the built CLI as a subprocess against the fixture project at tests.e2e.fixturevia vitest, so changes are validated against a realistic shape rather than mocks. Tests must pass before a change is merged or the package is published.

Pick the test file that owns the surface you changed:

  • New or modified query command (search, pick, context, ls, docs, flows, registry, ...) → tests.commands. Cover both plain and JSON output, and any new flag.
  • Build output or graph shape (new node kind, new edge type, metadata field, summary) → tests.build, with structural invariants in tests.graph-integrity.
  • Init / scaffolding flow (new generated files, prompts, defaults, idempotency) → tests.init.

If the new feature needs fixture data the existing one doesn't provide — a new registry primitive, a new doc shape, a new boundary kind, a new external — extend tests.e2e.fixture first, then write the test. A passing test that doesn't actually exercise the new surface is worse than no test.

docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-ski.content:
Every file under `.claude/skills/tskb*/` — both `SKILL.md` and any `references/*.md` — is generated by the docs build. Hand edits to those files are overwritten on the next run, so treat the generated tree as build output, not source.

The source of truth lives in two modules. cli.utils.content-builderowns the markdown bodies: skill prose, the reference-file contents, and the Copilot instructions. cli.utils.skill-generatorwrites those bodies to disk, including the frontmatter (name, description, allowed-tools) and the per-skill `references/` directory. To change a skill's content, frontmatter, or its set of references, edit one of those two modules and rebuild — never the generated Markdown.

docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-rea.explains: When must the npm README.md be updated?
docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-rea.content: The package in TSKB.Package.Rootis published to npm. npm.README.mdis the primary documentation users see on the registry. When structural changes are made to the consumer-facing surface — tskb.clicommands, runtime.jsxcomponents, runtime.registry, runtime primitives - $ tskb.runtime, type interfaces, build output format, or generated artifacts ( cli.utils.skill-generator, cli.utils.copilot-instructions) — npm.README.mdin TSKB.Package.Rootmust be updated to reflect them.

docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-pan.explains: How are explorer panel views structured?
docs/src/tskb/constraints/constraint-pan.content:
Every panel state in the explorer is a View class held in the explorer.spa.router stack. explorer.spa.doc-panelrenders whichever view is on top — it has no view-specific markup of its own. New panel surfaces (docs list, settings page, flow inspector) must be added as Views, not as new methods on the panel or on explorer.spa.Router.

A view class must:

  • Live in tskb.explorer.app.router.viewsand implement the View interface from explorer.spa.router-types: a route getter, renderHeader() , and renderBody() .
  • Expose a static prefix and a parse(rest, deps) factory, where deps is explorer.spa.NodeRefHooks. explorer.spa.Routercalls the factory to rebuild the view from location.hash when the user hits browser back or opens a bookmarked URL — without a caller to construct it. explorer.spa.RefsViewis the reference implementation.
  • Read from explorer.spa.NodeRefHooksduring renderHeader() and renderBody() , not in the constructor. A view restored from the URL may render before its data has loaded. Reading at render time means the view can repaint when explorer.spa.panelRouterrefresh() fires and the data arrives.
  • Call explorer.spa.wireRefson every element you render. explorer.spa.doc-panelhas no link wiring — views handle that themselves.

Reusable UI belongs in tskb.explorer.app.router.components— currently explorer.spa.accordion( explorer.spa.renderAccordion) and explorer.spa.ref-links. If two views duplicate markup or behavior, extract it there. A view is an addressable state; a component is shared rendering or wiring.

Why this matters: the URL hash always reflects what the user is looking at. If a view reads data in the constructor, a hash-restored view silently shows stale or empty content. If new states bypass explorer.spa.Router, the hash stops updating and back-navigation breaks.

docs/src/tskb/cli/logging.tskb.tsx.explains: How does the CLI route log output and what controls verbosity?
docs/src/tskb/cli/logging.tskb.tsx.content:
CLI Logger

cli.utils.loggeris a module-level singleton that routes all CLI output to stderr, keeping stdout clean for query command results.

Design

  • All log output goes to stderr via process.stderr.write — never stdout
  • Configured once at startup in cli.indexvia configure( { verbose } ), driven by the --verbose CLI flag
  • Log functions: info() always prints, verbose() only when --verbose is set, error() for errors, time() prints a label then returns a closure that logs elapsed ms at verbose level

Output Modes

Query commands (search, pick, context, ls, docs) support three output modes on stdout:

  • Default: pretty-printed JSON (human-readable)
  • --optimized: compact JSON (no whitespace, smaller payload)
  • --plain: structured plain text optimized for AI assistants — no JSON overhead, ~30% fewer tokens while preserving all semantic content. Uses plainOut() in the logger

Verbosity

  • Default: cli.commands.buildshows key stages and summary counts. Query commands produce output only on stdout, nothing on stderr
  • --verbose: build adds timing per pipeline stage, path resolution stats, and file paths. Query commands log "Loaded graph from ..." on stderr

docs/src/tskb/cli/cli-overview.tskb.tsx.explains: CLI structure: commands (init, build, search, pick, context, ls, docs, flows) and utils (output generators, content builder, logger)
docs/src/tskb/cli/cli-overview.tskb.tsx.content:
CLI

Located in tskb.cli. Entry point: cli.index— parses arguments, routes to command handlers.

Commands

In tskb.cli.commands:

  • cli.commands.init: Interactive scaffolder — creates docs folder, tsconfig, starter doc, build script
  • cli.commands.build: Full pipeline — files → TypeScript program → extraction → graph → outputs
  • cli.commands.search: Fuzzy search across all node types, returns ranked JSON results
  • cli.commands.pick: Resolve any node by ID or path, returns type-specific context
  • cli.commands.ls: List folder hierarchy with depth control, includes essential docs
  • cli.commands.flows: List or search flows sorted by priority (constraint → essential → supplementary)

Utils

In tskb.cli.utils:

  • cli.utils.content-builder: Produces two markdown bodies — query body and update body (syntax + session triggers) — consumed by skill and instructions generators
  • cli.utils.skill-generator: Generates two Claude Code skills — tskb and tskb-update
  • cli.utils.copilot-instructions: Generates two Copilot instructions — tskb and tskb-update
  • cli.utils.graph-finder: Finds .tskb/graph.json from cwd, used by search/pick/ls
  • cli.utils.logger: Stderr-only logger with info/verbose/error/time — configured once at startup via --verbose flag
  • cli.utils.resolve-node: Resolves any identifier to a graph node via cli.utils.resolve-node.resolveNode(exact ID → path → nearest parent folder). Also provides edge helpers: getNodeEdges, findReferencingDocs, findParent, findAllNodesById.

edges.label: discovers test files, registers global setup
builds fixture graph once via tskb build
provides CLI runners and graph loader to all test files
orchestrates the test: arranges temp dir, runs init, asserts output
copies fixture src/ into the temp directory
runs tskb init --yes in the temp directory
Orchestrates the pipeline: file discovery, extraction, graph building, output generation
Scans TypeScript AST for global tskb namespace declarations (Folders, Modules, Exports, Terms)
Receives completed registry; traverses JSX trees to extract content, references, relations, and flows — embeds data-node-display in reference links using registry-resolved paths
Merges registry and docs into a unified graph with nodes, edges, and hierarchy
Transforms the graph into DOT format for Graphviz visualization
entry point: instantiates the transformer, indexes edges by source and target, then runs the phases below
builds the meta chunk: root folder, top folders, docs, flows, terms, externals, and cross-lane edges
ExplorerApp.mount() runs the panel-router setup block after canvas and renderer are ready
new DocPanel(router): shell subscribes so every stack change re-renders the panel
registerView(RefsView, deps): binds deps into a factory closure keyed by RefsView.prefix
init({ syncHash: true }): starts hashchange listening and restores any view in the current hash
If location.hash is non-empty, restoreFromHash() calls the factory to rebuild the view
onChipClick(node, 'docs' | 'flows'): constructs new RefsView(nodeId, kind, deps)
router.push(view): identical-route guard skips dupes; otherwise appends to the stack
notify() fans out the new top-of-stack and canGoBack to every subscriber
onViewChange clears title/body, calls view.renderHeader(title, ctx) then view.renderBody(body)
renderHeader and renderBody write HTML then call wireRefs on their own elements
